explanation.text: Clouds drift and flow across the sky, and Euphemia's horses have swirling manes and seem to run freely across the paper. The simile therefore makes the drawings appear full of motion.
Marisa sharpens her pencil, uses a clean sheet, pictures a perfect horse, and feels certain she can finally draw it. These details emphasize her hope that this attempt will succeed.
Marisa's phrase mocks the horse's failed ears and noodlelike legs as she crosses out and hides the drawing. This self-critical description therefore conveys her frustrated, discouraged tone.
Marisa continues dwelling on the failed drawing through dinner instead of letting it go. “Brooded” therefore emphasizes her worry and insecurity about whether she can draw well.

The sunset makes even the familiar, scraggly horses look special, prompting Marisa to sit down and observe them closely. This unexpected beauty therefore becomes the inspiration for her successful drawing.
Because Marisa carefully notices the real horses' curved muscles, facial shadows, and shining backs, she sees their forms accurately and makes the final drawing different.
At first Marisa copies Euphemia's imagined horse and repeatedly fails; then she observes the real horses and uses pastels. The new approach works, so she feels proud enough to sign her own name.

The father exaggerates the pearl's worth as a “gem for the king,” but Ramón confidently points out its hidden flaw. His later smile and praise show that he was testing Ramón's confidence and judgment.
Calling it suitable only “for a poor king,” Ramón rejects his father's high valuation and then identifies the muddy flaw. This confident, accurate judgment shows that his expertise is developing.
The father imagines storms drowning them both and warns Ramón about sharks, devilfish, and giant mantas. These dangers show that his reluctance comes from concern for Ramón's safety.

Ramón changes his request from diving to performing the safer deck work of pulling baskets and tending ropes. This compromise is therefore a gradual attempt to overcome his father's objections and gain permission.
After Ramón offers to remain on deck, he sees his father's face begin to soften. That visible shift therefore signals the turning point from firm refusal toward the final decision to let Ramón come.

The father initially treats Ramón as vulnerable, but he praises Ramón's pearl judgment and softens when Ramón proposes useful deck work. By inviting him along, he therefore begins to acknowledge Ramón's maturity.

Ramón's dialogue reveals his confidence about the flawed pearl, his desire to join the voyage, and each argument he uses to persuade his father. Their exchange most directly develops his point of view.

The opening lists spider silk's strength, lightness, and flexibility, then describes possible medical, clothing, sports, building, and aerospace uses. These applications explain why scientists want a way to produce much more silk.

Spiders need isolation and may eat one another, while goats are friendly, easy to manage, and large enough to outproduce many spiders. Their size and temperament therefore make goats easier to raise for silk production.

Turner noticed that the spider body parts that produce silk closely resemble the goat parts that produce milk. This structural similarity therefore led him to believe goats could make spider-silk proteins.

The spinning machine repeatedly stretches the silk, causing its protein building blocks to line up and lock together. That repeated stretching gives the thread the strength required for delicate surgical uses.

The goats live in a normal environment with ample room to roam and play, and they are described as healthy, curious, energetic, calm, and happy. These details therefore reassure readers that the animals are treated well.

Scientists place spider genes in Nubian goats, causing the goats' milk to contain spider-silk proteins that can be separated and spun. This genetic alteration and its result therefore form the article's central idea.

Spider silk is stronger than steel yet light and flexible enough for products ranging from surgical stitches to bridges. Those unusual and useful qualities explain the entire effort to produce it in large quantities.

Nina first stares at the substitute's unusual bracelets, but the name Charlemagne makes her imagine a connection to medieval royalty and call it thrilling. Learning the name therefore increases her fascination.

Ms. Charlemagne asks students to let creativity guide them and discover what appears on the page. This therefore introduces Nina, who normally plans a finished image first, to a more spontaneous view of art.
“Chirped” suggests a bright, lively, upbeat voice. The word matches Ms. Charlemagne's enthusiastic invitation to let colors and pictures choose themselves, demonstrating her cheerful outlook.

Nina has been staring helplessly at the low score, but she suddenly realizes she controls what appears in her drawing and can erase it. This insight therefore signals a change from anxious fixation to imaginative agency.

Nina realizes that within her artwork she is “the lord” and can transform the score that troubles her. This quotation supports the theme that creative expression can provide control and a new perspective.

Ms. Charlemagne has beaded braids, one feather earring, clattering bracelets, a gleeful manner, and an unconventional approach to art. This distinctive combination supports the conclusion that she has a unique personality.

Nina draws the feared scene of her mother seeing the low score, then discovers she can erase and transform that score within the picture. Her art therefore helps her confront and work through her anxiety.
